preamble.summary: Those who trust in the LORD are compared to Mount Zion — immovable, permanent, settled forever. As mountains surround Jerusalem, so the LORD surrounds his people. The psalm then addresses the danger of wickedness gaining power over the righteous and prays that the upright will not be tempted to stretch their hands toward wrongdoing. It closes with a prayer for peace on Israel.
preamble.remarkable: The psalm builds its theology on geography. The pilgrim approaching Jerusalem can see the physical reality the psalm describes: mountains encircle the city on every side — the Mount of Olives to the east, hills to the north, south, and west. Jerusalem sits in a bowl of mountains. The psalmist takes this visible fact and makes it a metaphor for divine protection: as the mountains surround Jerusalem, so the LORD surrounds his people. The image is not of a single shield but of an encircling embrace — protection from every direction, permanent as the geology itself.
preamble.friction: Verse 3 introduces a complex theological concern: the scepter of wickedness (shevet haresha) must not rest on the land allotted to the righteous, lest the righteous themselves be corrupted and reach toward injustice. This is not a simple good-versus-evil framework — it acknowledges that oppression can deform the oppressed, that sustained injustice can tempt righteous people into wrongdoing. The psalm prays against this corruption, not merely against the oppressors.
preamble.connections: The comparison of the faithful to Mount Zion connects to Isaiah 28:16, where God lays a foundation stone in Zion. The phrase shalom al Yisrael ('peace on Israel') closing the psalm echoes Psalm 128:6 and is nearly identical to the closing of Psalm 128. The concern about the 'scepter of wickedness' echoes the warnings of Deuteronomy about foreign rule corrupting Israel's covenant faithfulness.

verses.text_hebrew: שִׁ֗יר הַֽמַּ֫עֲל֥וֹת הַבֹּטְחִ֥ים בַּיהוָ֑ה כְּהַר־צִ֝יּ֗וֹן לֹא־יִ֝מּ֗וֹט לְעוֹלָ֥ם יֵשֵֽׁב׃
verses.text_kjv: They that trust in the LORD shall be as mount Zion, which cannot be removed, but abideth for ever.
verses.rendering:
A song of ascents.
Those who trust in the LORD are like Mount Zion —
it cannot be shaken; it endures forever.
verses.expanded_rendering: The verb yimmot ('be shaken, be moved') is the same word used in Psalm 121:3 for the foot that will not slip. Mount Zion is the ultimate image of stability — bedrock, immovable, geological permanence. The comparison says that trust in God gives human life the stability of a mountain. The phrase le-olam yeshev ('forever it sits/endures') uses yashav ('to sit, to dwell, to remain') — the mountain does not merely resist shaking, it remains seated, settled, established. This is not passive survival but active, permanent presence.
translator_notes: Mount Zion (har Tsiyon) originally referred to the Jebusite fortress David captured (2 Samuel 5:7) and came to represent the temple mount and, by extension, God's chosen dwelling place. The pilgrims approaching Jerusalem would see this mountain and understand the psalm's metaphor viscerally.

verses.text_hebrew: יְרוּשָׁלַ֗͏ִם הָרִים֮ סָבִ֢יב לָ֫הּ֥ וַ֭יהוָה סָבִ֣יב לְעַמּ֑וֹ מֵ֝עַתָּ֗ה וְעַד־עוֹלָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: As the mountains are round about Jerusalem, so the LORD is round about his people from henceforth even for ever.
verses.rendering:
Jerusalem — mountains surround her,
and the LORD surrounds his people
from this moment and forever.
translator_notes: The word saviv ('around, surrounding') appears twice, creating a direct equation: mountains surround Jerusalem; the LORD surrounds his people. The temporal extension me-attah ve-ad olam ('from now and until forever') elevates the geographical observation into a permanent theological truth. The visible mountains will erode over eons; God's surrounding presence will not.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֤י לֹ֨א יָנ֡וּחַ שֵׁ֤בֶט הָרֶ֗שַׁע עַ֗ל גּוֹרַ֥ל הַצַּדִּיקִ֑ים לְמַ֡עַן לֹא־יִשְׁלְח֖וּ הַצַּדִּיקִ֣ים בְּעַוְלָתָ֗ה יְדֵיהֶֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: For the rod of the wicked shall not rest upon the lot of the righteous; lest the righteous put forth their hands unto iniquity.
verses.rendering:
For the scepter of wickedness will not rest
on the land allotted to the righteous,
so that the righteous will not stretch out
their hands toward injustice.
verses.expanded_rendering: The shevet haresha ('scepter/rod of wickedness') is a symbol of ruling authority exercised in corruption. The goral hatsaddiqim ('the lot/allotment of the righteous') refers to the land assigned by divine distribution — the inheritance of those who live rightly. The psalm's concern is not merely that the wicked oppress the righteous, but that prolonged oppression will corrupt the righteous themselves: lema'an lo yishlechu hatsaddiqim be-avlatah yedeihem ('so that the righteous will not send their hands toward injustice'). This is a psychologically sophisticated observation — sustained oppression can tempt the oppressed to adopt the methods of their oppressors.
translator_notes: The word goral ('lot') refers to the system of land distribution by casting lots, as in Joshua 14-21. The 'lot of the righteous' is their divinely assigned inheritance. The threat is that foreign or wicked rule over this land will demoralize its inhabitants.

The phrase be-avlatah ('toward injustice') uses avlah, a general term for moral crookedness or perversion of justice. The fear is not a specific sin but a general drift toward wrongdoing under pressure.

key_terms.semantic_range: rod, staff, scepter, tribe, ruling authority
key_terms.note: shevet has a wide range: a shepherd's rod, a king's scepter, a tribal designation. Here it represents governing authority — specifically, corrupt authority imposed on those who do not deserve it. The 'scepter of wickedness' is tyrannical rule.

verses.text_kjv: Do good, O LORD, unto those that be good, and to them that are upright in their hearts.
verses.rendering:
Do good, LORD, to those who are good,
and to those who are upright in their hearts.
translator_notes: The prayer heitivah YHWH lattovim ('do good to the good') creates a wordplay between the verb hetiv ('do good') and the adjective tovim ('good'). The psalm asks for correspondence between character and consequence — let good people receive good treatment. The added qualification velisharim belibotam ('and to the upright in their hearts') specifies that the uprightness is internal, not merely behavioral.

verses.text_hebrew: וְהַמַּטִּ֤ים עַֽקַלְקַלּוֹתָ֗ם יוֹלִיכֵ֣ם יְ֭הוָה אֶת־פֹּעֲלֵ֣י הָאָ֑וֶן שָׁ֝ל֗וֹם עַל־יִשְׂרָאֵֽל׃
verses.text_kjv: As for such as turn aside unto their crooked ways, the LORD shall lead them forth with the workers of iniquity: but peace shall be upon Israel.
verses.rendering:
But those who turn to crooked paths —
the LORD will lead them away with the evildoers.
Peace on Israel.
verses.expanded_rendering: The contrast is sharp: the upright receive good; those who turn aside (hammattim) to their crooked ways (aqalqallotam) are led away with the workers of iniquity (po'alei ha-aven). The word aqalqallot ('crooked paths, twisting ways') is an intensified form — extremely crooked, tortuous. These are not people who stumble but people who deliberately choose twisted routes. The final phrase shalom al Yisrael ('peace on Israel') lands like a benediction after the warning. It is a liturgical closing that re-centers the congregation on hope: whatever happens to the wicked, may Israel as a whole know peace.
translator_notes: The closing shalom al Yisrael functions as a liturgical formula, similar to 'amen.' It appears again at the end of Psalm 128, bracketing the central Songs of Ascents with a prayer for peace. The shift from warning to blessing is abrupt but deliberate — the psalm refuses to end on judgment.
